content.s1_claims_discipline_internal:
# TAGS: gluten, celiac, coeliac, NCGS, non-celiac gluten sensitivity, wheat allergy, wheat intolerance, gliadin, gluten sensitivity, gluten intolerance, gluten free, gluten reaction, gluten elimination, tTG-IgA, anti-tissue transglutaminase, anti-gliadin antibody, anti-endomysial, EMA-IgA, HLA-DQ2, HLA-DQ8, celiac panel, celiac genetic test, celiac blood test, gluten biopsy, zonulin, zonulin elevated, intestinal permeability gluten, leaky gut gluten, tight junction gluten, gut barrier gluten, gluten autoimmune, molecular mimicry gluten, gluten Hashimoto, gliadin TPO cross-reactivity, gluten thyroid, gluten malabsorption, gluten iron deficiency, gluten anemia, gluten B12, gluten vitamin D, gluten calcium, gluten reintroduction, gluten tolerance restoration, gluten ladder, sourdough tolerance, einkorn gluten, spelt tolerance, ancient wheat, gluten challenge, gluten bloating, gluten fatigue, gluten brain fog, gluten joint pain, gluten skin, gluten eczema, gluten psoriasis, gluten hair loss, gluten anxiety, dermatitis herpetiformis, gluten protocol, gluten 5R, gut repair gluten, L-glutamine gut barrier, gluten supplement protocol, gluten elimination diet, AIP diet gluten, gluten follow-up, gluten monitoring
# CATEGORY: Complex Conditions
# USE FOR: Any patient presenting with suspected gluten sensitivity, celiac disease, wheat allergy, gluten-related digestive symptoms, or asking whether they can eat gluten again. This is the PRIMARY routing destination for all gluten-related queries. Do not reconstruct this content from 12_GUT or 22_AUTOIMMUNE — load this file.
# COUPLED_SYSTEMS: 12_GUT, 22_AUTOIMMUNE, 04_NUTRITION_DIET, 36_TESTING_GUIDE, 57_THYROID_COMPLETE, 11_IMMUNE, 65_BIOMARKER_ATLAS, 61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT, 03_GENETIC, 27_PEDIATRIC
# RED_FLAGS: Severe unintentional weight loss, blood in stool, persistent vomiting, neurological symptoms (gluten ataxia), new refractory anemia despite supplementation → specialist referral immediately
# TRIAGE_QUESTIONS: Was celiac ever confirmed by biopsy? Are you currently gluten-free? When did symptoms start? Any family history of celiac? Any autoimmune conditions diagnosed?
# IV_ROLE: IV is NOT first-line for gluten conditions. Exception: documented malabsorption with low ferritin + B12 + VitD despite oral supplementation → IV repletion (Myers or targeted) while gut heals. Glutathione IV as secondary support for liver/oxidative load during transition.
# INTERVENTION_PRIORITY: Diagnosis first (distinguish 3 types) → Elimination protocol → Gut repair (5R) → Micronutrient repletion → Zonulin monitoring → Reintroduction (NCGS only)
# TELECONSULT_LIMITS: CAN do remotely: full differential diagnosis, lab panel ordering, elimination protocol, 5R protocol, supplement protocol, zonulin follow-up, reintroduction guidance. CANNOT do remotely: endoscopy/biopsy for celiac confirmation, skin biopsy for dermatitis herpetiformis.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GLUTEN SENSITIVITY — CELIAC — NCGS
# Complete Clinical Reference: Diagnosis | Protocol | Resolution
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 === SECTION 1: CLAIMS DISCIPLINE [INTERNAL_ONLY] ===
## THE THREE TYPES — NOT THE SAME THING

When people say "I'm allergic to gluten," they usually mean one of three very
different conditions. Getting the right diagnosis changes everything about how
we treat it — including whether you'll be able to eat gluten again one day.

---

### TYPE 1: CELIAC DISEASE (Autoimmune — Genetic)

**What it is:**
Your immune system attacks your own intestinal lining every time you eat gluten.
It's genetic (HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8 genes), autoimmune, and permanent.

**Key fact:** You were born with the genetic predisposition. A trigger
(infection, pregnancy, surgery, major stress) "turned it on."

**Can it be reversed?** No. The immune attack is hardwired.
Strict gluten-free diet for life. But symptoms can be 100% resolved
and the intestine heals completely on a strict GF diet.

**Who has it:** ~1% of the global population. Many undiagnosed.

---

### TYPE 2: NON-CELIAC GLUTEN SENSITIVITY / NCGS (Acquired — Reversible)

**What it is:**
Your gut barrier has been damaged (by dysbiosis, antibiotics, stress, infections,
processed food). This lets partially digested gluten proteins (gliadin) leak into
your bloodstream. Your immune system reacts to the intruder. This is NOT genetic.
It's a consequence of gut health breakdown.

**The mechanism:**
```
Gut barrier damage
    → Zonulin released → Tight junctions open → Leaky gut
    → Gliadin enters bloodstream undigested
    → Immune system sees it as foreign → Sensitization
    → "Gluten sensitivity" symptoms appear
```

**Can it be reversed?** YES — in the majority of cases.
Heal the gut barrier → zonulin normalizes → tight junctions close →
gluten can be properly digested again → tolerance restored.

**Timeline:** 3-6 months of structured protocol for most patients.

**Who has this:** The MAJORITY of people who say they're "gluten sensitive."
This is the most common and most treatable form.

---

### TYPE 3: WHEAT ALLERGY (IgE-Mediated — Immunological)

**What it is:**
A classic IgE-mediated allergic response to wheat proteins (not just gliadin).
Can cause immediate reactions: hives, throat tightening, anaphylaxis in severe cases.

**Can it be reversed?** Sometimes, with allergen immunotherapy. Requires allergist.

**Who has this:** Less common. More frequent in children (many outgrow it).

---

## SYMPTOMS BY TYPE

| Symptom | Celiac | NCGS | Wheat Allergy |
|---------|--------|------|---------------|
| Bloating/gas | Yes | Yes | Sometimes |
| Diarrhea/constipation | Yes | Yes | Sometimes |
| Brain fog | Yes | Yes | No |
| Fatigue | Yes | Yes | No |
| Joint pain | Yes | Yes | No |
| Skin rash (DH) | Yes (specific) | Sometimes | Hives (different) |
| Anemia | Yes | Sometimes | No |
| Immediate reaction | No | No | Yes |
| Thyroid connection | Strong (Hashimoto) | Moderate | No |
| Neurological | Yes (ataxia) | Sometimes | No |

---

## THE GOOD NEWS FOR MOST PEOPLE

If you don't have confirmed celiac disease (biopsy), there is a real chance
that with the right protocol, you'll be able to eat good-quality sourdough bread
again in 3-6 months.

The protocol works by fixing what caused the sensitivity in the first place —
not just managing the symptom.

---

 === SECTION 2: PATIENT COMMUNICATION [PATIENT_OK] ===
## STEP 1: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS PROTOCOL

### 1.1 — TRIAGE QUESTIONS (ask before ordering any labs)

Ask in this order:
1. Was celiac ever confirmed by intestinal biopsy? (not just blood test)
2. Is the patient currently on a gluten-free diet? (if yes: blood tests may be falsely negative)
   → If GF >6 weeks: gluten challenge needed for accurate testing (8 weeks, 2-3 slices/day)
3. Any first-degree relative with confirmed celiac?
4. Any diagnosed autoimmune condition? (Hashimoto, T1D, lupus → higher celiac risk)
5. Onset: sudden after an event (infection, pregnancy, antibiotics course) → NCGS likely
   vs progressive since childhood → celiac more likely

### 1.2 — DIAGNOSTIC DECISION FORK

```
PATIENT: "I react to gluten / I'm gluten sensitive"
    │
    ├─ Biopsy confirmed celiac? ──YES──→ [CELIAC PROTOCOL] Section 3
    │
    ├─ Biopsy NOT done ──────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                                             │
    │  ORDER: Celiac panel (see 1.3)                             │
    │                                                             ▼
    │  tTG-IgA POSITIVE + Total IgA normal                       │
    │  ──→ HIGH probability celiac → Refer gastro for biopsy    │
    │      → Start GF diet while awaiting biopsy                 │
    │      → [CELIAC PROTOCOL] Section 3                         │
    │                                                             │
    │  tTG-IgA NEGATIVE / borderline                             │
    │  ──→ Check: Was patient GF during test?                    │
    │      YES → Gluten challenge → retest at 8 weeks            │
    │      NO  → Celiac unlikely                                  │
    │           → Assess for NCGS (see 1.4)                     │
    │           → [NCGS PROTOCOL] Section 4                      │
    │                                                             │
    └─ Immediate reaction (hives, throat, anaphylaxis)           │
       ──→ Wheat allergy suspected                               │
       ──→ Refer allergist, order IgE wheat specific             │
           → NOT managed within Valentine scope alone            │
```

### 1.3 — CELIAC PANEL (standard)

| Test | Why | Notes |
|------|-----|-------|
| tTG-IgA (anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA) | Primary celiac screen | MOST SENSITIVE — order first |
| Total IgA | Validates tTG-IgA result | IgA deficiency (2% pop) = false negative tTG |
| Anti-DGP (deamidated gliadin peptide) IgG | Backup if IgA deficient | Use when total IgA low |
| Anti-endomysial IgA (EMA) | High specificity | Positive = celiac very likely |
| HbA1c, CBC, iron panel | Malabsorption screen | Anemia, iron deficiency common in celiac |
| VitD, B12, folate, calcium | Malabsorption panel | Standard in any celiac workup |

**CRITICAL:** All tests must be done ON A GLUTEN-CONTAINING DIET.
If patient is already GF → results will be falsely negative → document and note.

### 1.4 — NCGS ASSESSMENT (when celiac panel negative)

Clinical indicators supporting NCGS diagnosis:
- Symptoms clearly improve on GF diet AND worsen on gluten reintroduction
- tTG-IgA negative (or very low)
- No IgE-mediated reaction (no hives, no immediate response)
- History of gut disruption: antibiotics, GI infection, high stress period, processed food heavy diet
- Possible zonulin elevation (available via specialized labs, not standard)
- Often concurrent: dysbiosis, SIBO, candida, H. pylori (query 12_GUT)

### 1.5 — GENETIC TESTING (Optional but useful for family / diagnostic clarity)

HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ8 testing (genetic):
- Negative = celiac genetically excluded (>99% certainty) → NCGS by default
- Positive = genetic susceptibility confirmed, NOT diagnosis (30% of general pop carry it)
- Useful for: ruling out celiac in GF patients who can't do gluten challenge
- Lab: blood or saliva. Query 03_GENETIC for implementation.

---

## STEP 2 — ADDITIONAL CROSS-SYSTEM CHECKS (always run)

### Gluten × Thyroid (HIGH priority — Hashimoto link)

Molecular mimicry between gliadin and thyroid tissue (TPO).
Anti-gliadin antibodies can cross-react with thyroid → Hashimoto acceleration.
→ If patient has any thyroid symptoms or family history: order TPO + TG antibodies
→ If Hashimoto confirmed: gluten elimination is non-negotiable (see 57_THYROID_COMPLETE)

### Gluten × Nutrient Malabsorption (always assess)

```
Gluten damage → Villous atrophy → Reduced absorption surface
→ Check: Ferritin, Iron, TIBC (iron deficiency anemia)
→ Check: B12, Folate (megaloblastic anemia)
→ Check: VitD, Calcium (osteopenia risk, especially celiac >5 years)
→ Check: Magnesium (often low — drives muscle cramps, anxiety, sleep issues)
→ Check: Zinc (immune + gut repair impaired without it)
```
Use 65_BIOMARKER_ATLAS for individual marker interpretation.

### Gluten × Autoimmune Terrain

Celiac + autoimmune: T1D (10x risk), Hashimoto (3x risk), lupus, MS, RA.
If any autoimmune in history → query 22_AUTOIMMUNE for coupled management.
Gluten elimination reduces autoimmune flare frequency in many cases even without celiac.

### Gluten × Skin

Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH) = celiac skin manifestation. Itchy blistering rash
on elbows, knees, buttocks. Pathognomonic for celiac. If present → biopsy of skin
lesion (not gut) confirms celiac. Refer dermatology.
Query 21_SKIN for other gluten-skin connections (eczema, psoriasis triggers).

### Gluten × Cognitive / Neurological

"Gluten brain fog" is real — IL-6, TNF-α cross blood-brain barrier.
Gluten ataxia (rare): cerebellar ataxia caused by anti-gliadin antibodies attacking
Purkinje cells. If patient has balance issues + gluten sensitivity → neurology referral.
Query 15_COGNITIVE, 16_NEUROPSYCH.

---

## SECTION 3: CELIAC MANAGEMENT PROTOCOL

### What Valentine manages (non-specialist):

**A — Gluten-free diet coaching:**
- Strict GF: no wheat, rye, barley, spelt, kamut, triticale
- Hidden gluten: soy sauce, malt vinegar, many processed foods, medications
- Oats: technically GF but cross-contamination risk — use certified GF oats only
- Cross-contamination: separate toaster, cutting board, cookware
- Threshold: <10mg/day for mucosal healing (1 crumb can trigger reaction)

**B — Malabsorption correction (sequenced):**

Address in this order (sequence matters — VitD needed for Ca absorption):

| Phase | Nutrient | Target | Duration |
|-------|----------|--------|----------|
| 1st (weeks 1-4) | VitD3 + K2 | VitD 50-80 ng/mL | Loading then maintenance |
| 1st | Magnesium | RBC Mg optimal | Glycinate 300-400mg |
| 2nd (weeks 4-12) | Iron + VitC | Ferritin >50 µg/L | Bisglycinate form |
| 2nd | Zinc | Serum Zn optimal | 25-30mg with food |
| 3rd (weeks 8-16) | B12 | >500 pg/mL | Methylcobalamin sublingual |
| 3rd | Folate | >10 nmol/L | Methylfolate (not folic acid) |
| Ongoing | Calcium | Via food + small dose | If dairy also eliminated |

**C — Antibody monitoring:**

tTG-IgA should normalize at 6-12 months strict GF.
- At 6 months: expect 50% reduction minimum
- At 12 months: should be negative or near-negative
- Still elevated at 12 months → patient non-compliant (hidden gluten) OR refractory celiac
- If refractory → gastroenterologist required

**D — When to refer:**

- Persistent symptoms >12 months strict GF → rule out refractory celiac, associated conditions
- Dermatitis herpetiformis → dermatology for dapsone
- Neurological symptoms → neurology
- Pediatric celiac → pediatric gastroenterology (query 27_PEDIATRIC)

---

## SECTION 4: NCGS RESOLUTION PROTOCOL (Core of this file)

### The premise

NCGS is an acquired condition caused by gut barrier failure.
Fix the barrier → restore immune tolerance → gluten no longer problematic.
This is achievable in the majority of NCGS patients within 3-6 months.

### PHASE 1 — ELIMINATION + STABILIZATION (Weeks 1-8)

**Goal:** Remove the trigger, stop the immune activation, assess response.

**Diet:**
- Strict gluten elimination (same rules as celiac)
- Also eliminate: dairy (cross-reactive proteins — casein similar to gliadin)
- Keep elimination: minimum 8 weeks before any assessment
- Allowed: rice, quinoa, sweet potato, legumes, vegetables, quality proteins

**Concurrent gut assessment:**
- GI-MAP stool test → identify pathogens (SIBO, Candida, H. pylori, parasites)
- Address any findings with targeted antimicrobials BEFORE gut repair phase
- Query 12_GUT for 5R protocol and pathogen-specific protocols

**Baseline labs at week 0:**
- Celiac panel (tTG-IgA, total IgA)
- Zonulin (blood) — permeability marker — baseline reference
- Full malabsorption panel (ferritin, B12, VitD, zinc, magnesium)
- hsCRP, NLR — inflammatory baseline

**Expected at week 8:**
- Symptom improvement 60-80% in responders
- Non-responders: check hidden gluten sources, check other triggers (dairy, FODMAP, SIBO)

### PHASE 2 — GUT REPAIR (Weeks 4-20, overlapping with Phase 1)

**Goal:** Restore gut barrier integrity. Close the tight junctions. Normalize zonulin.

This is the 5R protocol applied with gluten focus:

**REMOVE** (ongoing from Phase 1)
- Gluten, dairy, processed foods, alcohol, NSAIDs
- Treat any active pathogen (SIBO, Candida — see GI-MAP results)

**REPLACE**
- Digestive enzymes with meals (especially protease — helps digest residual gliadin)
- HCl if low stomach acid (common with gut damage)

**REINOCULATE**
- Probiotics: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG + Bifidobacterium longum (evidence for gut barrier)
- Spore-based: Bacillus coagulans (more stable, good for leaky gut)
- Fermented foods: kefir (if dairy tolerated), kimchi, kombucha — introduce slowly
- Prebiotic fiber: inulin, FOS — but cautiously if SIBO present

**REPAIR — THE CRITICAL PHASE**

| Supplement | Dose | Mechanism | Duration |
|------------|------|-----------|----------|
| L-Glutamine | 10-20g/day | Primary fuel for enterocytes; closes tight junctions | 12-16 weeks |
| Zinc carnosine | 75-150mg/day | Mucosal healing, anti-inflammatory | 12 weeks |
| Colostrum | 500-2000mg/day | sIgA boost, growth factors for mucosa | 8-12 weeks |
| Quercetin | 500-1000mg/day | Tight junction stabilizer, mast cell stabilizer | 12 weeks |
| Slippery elm / DGL | Per product | Mucus layer protection | 8 weeks |
| Omega-3 (EPA+DHA) | 2-3g/day | Reduce intestinal inflammation | Ongoing |
| VitD3 | To achieve 60-80 ng/mL | VDR activation regulates tight junction proteins | Ongoing |

**Vitamin D is especially important:** VDR (Vitamin D Receptor) directly regulates
zonulin expression and tight junction protein (claudin, occludin) production.
Low VitD = harder to close tight junctions. This is mechanistic, not optional.

**REBALANCE**
- Stress management: HPA axis dysregulation perpetuates leaky gut (query 09_HPA_ADRENAL)
- Sleep: gut repair happens primarily during deep sleep
- Exercise: moderate only — high-intensity training can acutely increase permeability

### PHASE 3 — MONITORING (Weeks 8, 12, 20)

**Zonulin monitoring protocol:**

| Timepoint | Expected | Action |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| Baseline (Week 0) | May be elevated | Reference point |
| Week 8 | 20-30% reduction if protocol followed | Confirm compliance if no change |
| Week 12 | 40-60% reduction | Assess readiness for reintroduction |
| Week 20 | Near normal or normal | Proceed to Phase 4 |
| Still elevated at Week 20 | Check: hidden triggers, ongoing pathogen, stress | Extend Phase 2 |

**Symptom tracking:**
Digital symptom log (bloating 0-10, energy 0-10, cognition 0-10, bowel quality).
Minimum 30% improvement at week 8 = good responder → continue.
No improvement at week 8 → re-evaluate: hidden gluten, wrong diagnosis, SIBO untreated.

**Repeat labs at week 12:**
- Zonulin
- hsCRP (should be decreasing)
- Ferritin, VitD, B12, zinc (are they being absorbed now?)

### PHASE 4 — PROGRESSIVE REINTRODUCTION (After Week 20 minimum)

**Prerequisites before starting reintroduction:**
☐ Zonulin normalized (or near normal)
☐ All gut symptoms resolved
☐ No active pathogen (confirmed by GI-MAP if done)
☐ Inflammatory markers down (hsCRP <1.0, NLR <2.0)
☐ At least 16 weeks strictly GF
☐ Patient understands: this is a TEST, not a free pass

**Reintroduction protocol — gluten ladder:**

The principle: start with LEAST problematic gluten sources → progress toward modern wheat.
Modern industrial wheat has 40x more gluten than ancient varieties.

```
WEEK 1-2: Long-fermented sourdough (traditional, 24-48h ferment)
  → Fermentation partially pre-digests gliadin → easiest to tolerate
  → 1 slice every other day
  → Monitor: symptoms, energy, cognition for 48h after each exposure

WEEK 3-4: Einkorn or Emmer (ancient wheat varieties)
  → Lower gliadin content, different protein structure
  → 1-2 portions per week
  → Continue monitoring

WEEK 5-6: Spelt or Kamut (ancient, not modern wheat)
  → Step up from einkorn
  → Monitor

WEEK 7-8: Conventional organic wheat (good quality)
  → Only if previous steps passed without reaction
  → Test with whole wheat sourdough first

AVOID reaching for: Industrial white bread, processed wheat products, refined flour
→ Even healthy guts don't need these
```

**Reintroduction criteria: PASS vs FAIL**

PASS (continue to next rung):
- No symptoms for 48h post-exposure
- Energy stable
- Digestion normal
- No brain fog

FAIL (return to previous rung or full elimination):
- Any symptom recurrence
- Energy crash within 24h
- Digestive disruption
- Brain fog return

**If FAIL at sourdough rung:**
→ Gut is not ready → Extend Phase 2 by 4-6 weeks → Repeat zonulin
→ Do NOT push through — sensitization will worsen

### PHASE 4B — MAINTENANCE (Ongoing after successful reintroduction)

Not all patients can tolerate unlimited gluten even after healing.
Target: "Relaxed gluten tolerance" — occasional quality sourdough, ancient grains,
without reaction. Industrial wheat may remain problematic indefinitely.

Maintenance protocol:
- Continue probiotics (lower dose)
- Keep L-Glutamine 5g/day for 3 months post-reintroduction
- Monitor symptoms monthly
- Re-introduce zonulin test at 6 months post-reintroduction if symptomatic

---

## SECTION 5: LAB REFERENCE FOR GLUTEN CONDITIONS

### Diagnostic labs

| Test | Optimal | Celiac concern | Notes |
|------|---------|----------------|-------|
| tTG-IgA | <4 U/mL | >10 U/mL strongly positive | Must be on gluten diet |
| Total IgA | Normal range | If low: use anti-DGP IgG | 2% population IgA deficient |
| Anti-DGP IgG | Negative | Positive | Use if IgA deficient |
| EMA-IgA | Negative | Positive | High specificity for celiac |
| Zonulin (blood) | <35 ng/mL | >40 ng/mL elevated | Not yet standardized — use trending |
| HLA-DQ2/DQ8 | Negative = celiac excluded | Positive = susceptibility | Via 03_GENETIC |
| IgE wheat specific | <0.35 kU/L | >0.35 kU/L | For wheat allergy differential |

### Malabsorption panel (all gluten conditions)

| Marker | Optimal | Action if low |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| Ferritin | 50-100 µg/L | Iron bisglycinate + VitC |
| Iron | 80-120 µg/dL | As above |
| % Transferrin Sat | 25-35% | Iron protocol |
| B12 | >500 pg/mL | Methylcobalamin sublingual |
| Folate | >10 nmol/L | Methylfolate |
| VitD | 60-80 ng/mL | D3 + K2 loading |
| Calcium | 9.0-10.0 mg/dL | Via food + small dose supplement |
| Magnesium (RBC) | >2.2 mg/dL | Glycinate form |
| Zinc | 80-120 µg/dL | Zinc bisglycinate 25mg |

### Follow-up labs (NCGS protocol monitoring)

| Timepoint | Labs | Goal |
|-----------|------|------|
| Week 0 | Zonulin, hsCRP, NLR, malabsorption panel | Baseline |
| Week 8 | Zonulin, hsCRP, symptoms | 20-30% zonulin reduction |
| Week 12 | Full panel repeat | 40-60% zonulin reduction, nutrients improving |
| Week 20 | Zonulin, readiness assessment | Zonulin normal → proceed to reintroduction |
| Post-reintro 6mo | Zonulin, symptoms | Verify maintained tolerance |

---

## SECTION 6: QUICK REFERENCE

### Differential in 60 seconds
```
Biopsy confirmed celiac?    YES → Celiac Protocol (GF life + malabsorption)
Positive tTG-IgA?           YES → Probable celiac → refer gastro
Immediate IgE reaction?     YES → Wheat allergy → allergist
Everything else?            → NCGS → 5R + repair + reintroduction possible
```

### NCGS Resolution Timeline
```
Week 0:    Baseline labs + GI-MAP + strict elimination starts
Week 1-8:  5R protocol + gut repair supplements
Week 8:    First zonulin check — 20-30% reduction expected
Week 12:   Labs repeat — nutrients improving, symptoms resolving
Week 16:   Continue repair if zonulin not yet normal
Week 20:   Zonulin normal → green light for reintroduction ladder
Week 20+:  Progressive reintroduction: sourdough → ancient grains → modern wheat
Week 28+:  Maintenance — relaxed gluten tolerance achieved (majority of patients)
```

### Key supplements for gut barrier (doses)
```
L-Glutamine:   10-20g/day    [primary — do not skip]
Zinc carnosine: 150mg/day    [mucosal healing]
VitD3:         To 60-80ng/mL [tight junction regulation — critical]
Quercetin:     1000mg/day    [tight junction stabilizer]
L. rhamnosus GG: per product [barrier probiotic]
Omega-3:       3g EPA+DHA    [anti-inflammatory]
```

### Gluten ladder (reintroduction order)
```
1. Long-fermented sourdough (24-48h)  [EASIEST]
2. Einkorn / Emmer (ancient wheat)
3. Spelt / Kamut
4. Organic conventional wheat sourdough
5. Modern wheat products              [HARDEST — may remain avoided]
```

### Files to query in parallel
```
12_GUT          → 5R protocol detail, SIBO/Candida/H.pylori treatment
22_AUTOIMMUNE   → If Hashimoto or other autoimmune concurrent
57_THYROID      → Gluten-thyroid cross-reactivity, TPO antibodies
36_TESTING_GUIDE → Full panel ordering
65_BIOMARKER_ATLAS → Individual marker interpretation
61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT → Overall terrain status with gut as axis
03_GENETIC      → HLA-DQ2/DQ8 testing
27_PEDIATRIC    → Celiac in children
```
content.s2_patient_communication_public: This topic is part of our integrative health knowledge base. We can explain the concepts and help you structure next steps. For safety, any diagnosis or medication decisions must be confirmed by a clinician with appropriate exams/labs.

content.s4_limitations_failure_modes_internal: - Reference content may not apply to every patient. Always individualize by symptoms, labs, and red flags.
